author: @montec6113
text:
Hi Rob! I know this might be unrelated to the video, but I think this would be a great idea for your channel.

I suggest making a self replicating lathe. What I mean by this is making a lathe that could recreate any part of itself (with exception of elecric motor and controllers). I find the idea of self replication fascinating and I am curious - how small and simple a self replicating inorganic organism could get.

The problem that is constantly encountered is generational degradation. A tool that builds another tool will do so in a less accurately than it is built itself. A solution to this is making a perfect flat surface that can be used to calibrate everything. You can create that surface by rubbing three different surfaces against each other, making them perfectly flat.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting idea mate - thanks for the suggestion

replies.author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
replies.text:
"A tool that builds another tool will do so in a less accurately than it is built itself."

Sorry, but this is *not* the case.  If it were, it would never have been possible to build machines with higher tolerances than the ones which were used to create them.  The capacity to measure precisely to beyond the tolerance required is usually the limiting factor, and was so even in the days of Whitworth's codification of the three flats method.

In a lot of cases, precision is down to working practices - on a lathe one cannot precisely take a cut which is less deep than the combined flex and slop of the piece, the slide assembly and toolholder, and the lathe spindle.  If, for a given piece, that limit is, say, 10 microns, I can't reliably make a cut from 10.01mm to 10.005, or even 10.00.  However, I *can* hit 10.005 if I can quantify and measure that flex, and order my machining in order to reduce flex and take account of slop.

replies.author: @montec6113
replies.text:
@wibblywobblyidiotvision Thanks for informing me! This is really interesting. So you can get the exact same accuracy without a generational loss if you have a good measuring tool huh?

What about cylinders? How can we be sure they are accurate? By using the flatbed? If so, how?

replies.author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
@montec6113
replies.text: @wibblywobblyidiotvision Fascinating. Would a self replicating lathe be feasable? It would need to be made out of many parts due to the limits of what that lathe can spin. I'd imagine it would introduce inaccuracies?
